title: What part of me still believes money could run out?
subtitle: A fifteen-minute journal prompt for the running-out loop.
excerpt: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt explores which part of you still carries the running-out belief — even when the bills are paid and the fridge is full. Use after a tapping session, or on any day the running-out loop is audible underneath the day.
sourceNotes: From Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Journal-prompt practice as a self-inquiry tool is a public-domain technique documented across therapeutic and journaling traditions.

practice.whenToUse: Use after a tapping session on the running-out fear, or on any day the scarcity loop is running underneath normal life. Not a crisis tool — use when the mind is calm enough to write honestly.

content.text: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt locates the running-out belief — not the logical knowledge that the bills are paid, but the part that is tracking the threat anyway. Use it after a tapping session on the fear, or on any day the scarcity loop is audible underneath normal life.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
What part of me still believes money could suddenly disappear?
Write without editing. Notice where the belief lives — is it a voice, a physical sensation, a specific memory? What would that part need to feel safe? Ten to fifteen minutes.

If you have more time: when was the first moment you remember believing that money wasn't reliable? Was it a childhood experience, something you witnessed, a period when the money actually did run out? Write that memory in as much detail as it comes.

Close the notebook. One slow breath. You don't have to do anything with what you wrote today — the act of writing it out is the practice. If a particular line surprised you, mark the corner of the page and come back to it later.

From Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), where releasing fear of running out of money is the focus. Journal-prompt practice as a self-inquiry tool is a public-domain technique documented across therapeutic and journaling traditions.
